skill.name: bash-pro
skill.description: Master of defensive Bash scripting for production automation, CI/CD pipelines, and system utilities. Expert in safe, portable, and testable shell scripts.
skill.summary: Professional Bash scripting with defensive programming, testing, and CI/CD integration
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a prompt-only skill that provides guidance on defensive Bash scripting. Static analysis scanned 0 files (0 lines) and detected no suspicious patterns or risk factors. The skill contains no executable code and only provides instructional content for writing safe, production-grade shell scripts. No command injection, credential access, or malicious patterns detected.

content.value_statement: Writing safe, testable Bash scripts for automation requires defensive programming patterns. This skill provides expert guidance on strict error handling, secure input validation, testing with Bats, and CI/CD integration.
actual_capabilities: Generate defensive Bash scripts with strict error handling (set -Eeuo pipefail)
Implement safe argument parsing with getopts and input validation
Create comprehensive test suites using the Bats testing framework
Set up CI/CD pipelines with ShellCheck, shfmt, and automated testing
Apply modern Bash 5.x features for cleaner, more maintainable code
Design secure file operations with proper quoting and temporary resource cleanup
limitations: Does not provide pre-built scripts or templates - generates new code on demand
Does not execute or test scripts - only provides guidance on how to do so
Does not support Windows-native scripting (PowerShell tasks require different skill)
POSIX-only shell scripts are outside scope when Bash-specific features are needed

use_cases.title: DevOps Engineer Building CI/CD Scripts
use_cases.description: Create robust deployment and build scripts that handle errors gracefully, clean up temporary resources, and integrate with GitHub Actions for automated testing.

use_cases.title: Developer Writing System Utilities
use_cases.description: Build command-line tools with proper help documentation, argument validation, and logging that can be safely used in production environments.
use_cases.target_user: Software developers creating CLI utilities and automation

use_cases.title: Site Reliability Engineer Reviewing Shell Scripts
use_cases.description: Audit existing Bash scripts for security vulnerabilities, portability issues, and best practice compliance using ShellCheck and defensive programming patterns.
use_cases.target_user: SREs maintaining and hardening existing shell scripts

prompt_templates.title: Basic Script Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Bash script that [describe the task]. Include strict mode, argument parsing with getopts, proper error handling, and a --help option.
prompt_templates.scenario: Creating a simple automation script with proper structure

prompt_templates.title: CI/CD Pipeline Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that lints Bash scripts with ShellCheck, formats with shfmt, and runs Bats tests. Include matrix testing across multiple Bash versions.
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up automated testing for shell script projects

prompt_templates.title: Secure File Processing
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a Bash script that safely processes files from user input. Include proper quoting, temp file cleanup with traps, input validation, and NUL-safe file handling.

prompt_templates.title: Production Hardening Review
prompt_templates.prompt:
Review this Bash script for security issues, error handling gaps, and portability problems. Identify specific ShellCheck warnings and provide fixes:

[paste script here]
prompt_templates.scenario: Auditing and hardening existing shell scripts

output_examples.input: Write a script that backs up a directory with timestamped archives
output_examples.output: A complete Bash script with: set -Eeuo pipefail strict mode, getopts for -s (source) -d (destination) -h (help), timestamped backup filenames, tar with proper error handling, cleanup trap for temp files, dry-run mode with DRY_RUN variable, and comprehensive --help output

output_examples.input: Create a script to monitor disk usage and alert when above threshold
output_examples.output: A monitoring script with: df command parsing, threshold validation, structured logging with timestamps, syslog integration, graceful handling of missing commands, and configurable alert levels via environment variables

best_practices: Always enable strict mode at script start: set -Eeuo pipefail to catch errors and undefined variables
Quote all variable expansions: use "$var" not $var to prevent word splitting and globbing
Use traps for cleanup: trap 'rm -rf "$tmpdir"' EXIT ensures temp files are always removed
anti_patterns: Using for f in $(ls ...) - causes word splitting bugs; use find with -print0 and read -r -d '' instead
Ignoring exit codes with | pipefail disabled - always check set -o pipefail and verify critical command exits
Using eval on user input - severe security risk; use arrays for dynamic command construction instead

faq.question: What is strict mode in Bash?
faq.answer: Strict mode is a set of Bash options (set -Eeuo pipefail) that causes the shell to exit on errors, undefined variables, and pipe failures. It prevents silent failures and makes scripts more reliable.

faq.question: Why should I use getopts instead of manual argument parsing?
faq.answer: getopts is a built-in Bash utility that handles argument parsing correctly, supports short (-f) and long (--file) options, and prevents common bugs in manual parsing.

faq.question: How do I test Bash scripts?
faq.answer: Use the Bats (Bash Automated Testing System) framework to write unit tests. Bats provides a TAP-compatible test format similar to RSpec or Jest for shell scripts.

faq.question: What is ShellCheck?
faq.answer: ShellCheck is a static analysis tool that detects bugs, portablity issues, and security problems in shell scripts. It should be run on all production Bash scripts.

faq.question: Why use mktemp and traps?
faq.answer: mktemp creates safe temporary files with unpredictable names. Traps ensure cleanup code runs even when the script exits unexpectedly, preventing orphaned temp files.

faq.question: How do I make scripts portable between Linux and macOS?
faq.answer: Use #!/usr/bin/env bash shebang, check for GNU vs BSD tool differences (sed -i behavior), test on all target platforms, and avoid Bash-specific features when POSIX compliance is needed.
